title: The 100M Frame

content.statement: Asking what a $10M or $100M version of the business would require reveals which current systems are unsustainable.
content.question: What would the $10M or $100M version of this business refuse to keep doing?
content.equation: Scale Readiness = Future Vision × System Durability × Founder Independence
content.mental_model: Trying on a giant coat, you immediately see what does not fit.
content.business_lens: Use a future-size version of the company to pressure-test today’s decisions.
content.life_lens: Ask what your future self would need in place and stop building habits that cannot support that life.
sop: Choose a future business size, such as $10M or $100M
Ask what systems, people, margins, and offers that version would need
Upgrade one bottleneck at a time (see calculation, page 6)
content.explainer: The 100M Frame helps you stop solving problems only from today’s perspective. A process may work when there are five clients but collapse at fifty. A founder may be able to manually approve everything now, but that becomes impossible at scale. This question makes invisible weaknesses visible.
questions: What would the $10M or $100M version of this business refuse to keep doing?
Which process only works today because you personally hold it together?
If revenue 10x'd next year, what part of the business would you have to rebuild from scratch?

definitions.def: The slowest or most constrained step in a system.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Future Vision 7, System Durability 3, Founder Independence 2. Score = 7 times 3 times 2 = 42 out of a possible 1,000. Higher means today's business could actually support tomorrow's size. Lower means the systems you have now would collapse under 10x growth.
content.closing_statement: Build for the business you're becoming.

caption:
Principle Nº 249: The 100M Frame

Asking what a $10M or $100M version of the business would require reveals which current systems are unsustainable. The 100M Frame helps you stop solving problems only from today’s perspective.

Ask yourself: What would the $10M or $100M version of this business refuse to keep doing?
